food_name: Ayam Buah Keluak (Nonya Chicken with Black Nut and Spice Paste)
aliases: Ayam Buah Keluak (Nonya Chicken with Black Nut and Spice Paste)

english: Ayam Buah Keluak (Nonya Chicken with Black Nut and Spice Paste)

region_of_origin: Singapore and Malacca, Straits Chinese (Peranakan) heritage
food_subgroup: Braised chicken in rempah with fermented black nut paste

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Ayam Buah Keluak or Peranakan cuisine. Closest parent is braised chicken dish. No exact URI available; null assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for braised chicken dish with spice paste. Peranakan-specific preparation not represented in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Ayam Buah Keluak or Peranakan braised chicken. A00LC (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Ayam Buah Keluak or Peranakan cuisine dishes.

main_ingredients: buah keluak (fermented Pangium edule black nut)
rempah (spice paste: galangal, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, dried chillies, candlenut, turmeric)

serving.description: 1 plate with rice (2 chicken pieces + gravy + buah keluak paste + steamed white rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising 2 chicken pieces (~200 g), gravy (~100 g), buah keluak paste (~50 g), and steamed white rice (~200 g).

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Buah Keluak — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient profile with USDA SR Legacy component derivation for buah keluak paste and rempah spices
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo Database; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Ayam Buah Keluak. Micronutrient values for buah keluak paste and rempah components (galangal, lemongrass, candlenut, turmeric) cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy component entries. Values represent the complete dish excluding rice unless otherwise noted.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated for Ayam Buah Keluak dish (chicken + gravy + buah keluak paste, excluding rice) using analogous braised chicken with spice paste reference from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Buah Keluak. GI of 28 estimated based on analogous braised chicken dishes with spice paste from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is protein- and fat-dominant with moderate carbohydrate from rempah and buah keluak paste; low GI classification is well-supported. Glycemic load of 12.5 calculated per 550 g serving (excluding rice component). Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from belacan (shrimp paste) and seasoning sauces in rempah
hypertension.guidance: A standard serving of Ayam Buah Keluak (excluding rice) delivers approximately 480 mg sodium; combined with rice and condiments a full meal can reach 600–800 mg sodium, representing 26–35% of the 2,300 mg daily limit, so hypertensive individuals should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 28) of the dish itself; carbohydrate load increases substantially when served with steamed white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: The chicken and keluak gravy component is low GI (GI ~28) and protein/fat-dominant, making it suitable for blood glucose management; however, the standard serving includes ~200 g steamed white rice (~45 g carbohydrate, GI ~64), which raises the meal glycemic load to moderate-high, so individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing rice portion or substituting with lower-GI alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut oil or lard and cholesterol from chicken; partially offset by anti-inflammatory rempah spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 100 g of Ayam Buah Keluak provides 2.5 g saturated fat and 48 mg cholesterol; a full serving (350 g dish component) contributes approximately 8.75 g saturated fat, approaching 40% of the 20 g daily limit for cardiovascular health, so individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and request preparation with vegetable oil rather than lard.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory rempah spices (galangal, lemongrass, turmeric, candlenut) and iron from buah keluak paste
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The rempah contains galangal (COX-2 inhibitory properties), lemongrass (citral anti-inflammatory), turmeric (curcumin), and candlenut (omega-3 ALA), which collectively support anti-inflammatory dietary patterns; iron (~1.8 mg/100 g) benefits RA patients who are commonly iron-deficient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Buah Keluak (亚参黑果鸡) is widely regarded as the most emblematic dish of Singapore's Peranakan (Nonya) culinary heritage. The defining ingredient is buah keluak (Pangium edule), a large, black, hard-shelled nut from coastal mangrove forests in Southeast Asia. Raw buah keluak is toxic, containing cyanogenic glycosides; it must be fermented for at least 40 days in ash and banana leaves before the flesh inside is safe to eat. After fermentation, the black paste scraped from inside the shells has a deep, complex, earthy-bitter flavour with no substitute in world cuisine. The paste is mixed with rempah spices — galangal, lemongrass, shallots, garlic, dried chillies, candlenut, turmeric, and belacan (shrimp paste) — and either stuffed back into the shells for presentation or dissolved into the braising gravy. Chicken pieces are braised slowly in this rempah until the meat is tender and deeply infused with the dark, fragrant sauce. Tamarind water provides acidity and balances the richness of the keluak paste. The resulting dish is dark, intensely savoury-sour, and umami-rich. Nutritionally, the dish provides 142 kcal per 100 g, with 11.5 g protein, 7.5 g fat, and 8.0 g carbohydrate. Sodium is moderate at 320 mg/100 g from belacan and seasoning. The dish is low GI (GI ~28) when assessed without rice, making it suitable for blood glucose management as a standalone dish. It is gluten-free and dairy-free. Traditionally prepared for Chinese New Year, Peranakan weddings, and major celebrations. The dish is not halal in most traditional preparations due to belacan (shrimp paste) and occasional use of pork lard.
common_user_queries: ayam buah keluak GI glycemic index

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the designated primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy. GI estimated from analogous braised chicken dish in University of Sydney GI Database with confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Ayam Buah Keluak is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy for this composite dish preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the complete braised dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g from the HPB/USDA source derivation. GI of 28 is an analogous estimate based on braised chicken with spice paste reference from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Buah Keluak exists in published literature. Glycemic load of 12.5 is calculated for the complete 550 g serving excluding rice. The dish is not halal in most traditional preparations due to belacan (shrimp paste) and occasional use of pork lard. Vitamin A reported as 35.0 mcg RAE per 100 g derived from rempah spice components (turmeric, galangal, chilli). Selenium reported as 14.0 mcg/100 g from chicken meat component per USDA SR Legacy.
